raw_case:
A rare presentation of hematosalpinx with torsion in a thirteen year-old virgin: a case report


Abstract

A medical condition called hematosalpinx causes an accumulation of blood within the fallopian tube. It is usually seen in patients with ectopic pregnancy. Inflammatory disease of the pelvis, tubal cancer, pelvic trauma, and endometriosis are further causes. Here, we report a unique case of hematosalpinx with associated tubal torsion in a 13-year-old female lacking any previously reported contributing causes. She is celibate and presented with abdominal pain and fever. Beta-hcg was not present, and her menstrual cycle was regular. Pelvic ultrasound sonography revealed a large cyst and was suggestive of a right paraovarian cyst. An exploratory laparotomy was performed and a giant hematosalpinx was observed in an otherwise normal ovary. In conclusion, although very rare in adolescence, hematosalpinx must be considered in the differential diagnosis. This unusual instance highlights new concerns regarding the pathogenesis of hematosalpinx.

Introduction

Hematosalpinx is the accumulation of blood in fallopian tubes. It is a rare condition that results from a distended blood-filled tubal lumen with a reported incidence of 1.08% to 1.8% [1]. Ectopic pregnancy is the main factor contributing to this condition; however, other factors include pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), endometriosis, and pelvic trauma [1, 2]. Abdominal pain, unusual vaginal bleeding, and irregular menstruation are just a few of the symptoms that might appear with hematosalpinx, which has a number of underlying etiologies. One or both fallopian tubes may be affected by the disorder, which can be unilateral or bilateral [3].Isolated fallopian tube torsion is a rare cause of acute lower abdomen pain with a rare underlying etiology. This was a rare case of isolated right fallopian tube torsion with hematosalpinx. Following are some hypothesized causes of fallopian tube torsion: physiologic abnormalities, such as abnormal peristalsis or periovulatory spasm; hemodynamic abnormalities, such as long mesosalpinx, tortuous dilated tube (hydro- or hematosalpinx); tubal mass (tubal neoplasm); and adnexal mass (adjacent ovarian or paraovarian tumor), such as Sellheim hypothesis (sudnexal venous congestion); trauma; prior surgery; or illnesses including PID; pelvic adhesion; tubal ligation; gravidity; and an enlarged or uterus [4].We report a case of hematosalpinx with bleeding in a 13-year-old girl who had an isolated right fallopian tube that was effectively treated with a partial salpingectomy.

Case presentation

A 13-year-old celibate girl was referred to our hospital after enduring 2 months of abdominal pain in the right lumbar and right iliac area. She explains the pain started gradually, was intermittent, and radiates to her right leg in a spasmodic manner. Before approaching the hospital, she also had related nausea, vomiting, and fever for 5 days. Two years ago, she reached menarche; albeit having a regular cycle, she experienced dysmenorrhea and clots. She did, however, have burning micturition and heavy bleeding during periods. Upon self-examination, a felt mass grew larger over time. There were no observed extragenital abnormalities or comorbidities.Throughout the examination, she was conscious, cooperative, and well-oriented to the time, place, and person. There was pallor. The umbilicus was soft to the touch, centered in the abdomen, and free of any scars or pulsations. There was pain and a suprapubic mass in the lower right quadrant of the abdomen. Upon assessment, no further complications were found.An additional examination of the electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia. The ultrasound of the right ovary showed a large, clearly defined anechoic cystic lesion measuring 10.9 × 10.00 × 11.1 cm (volume: 641 cc), with only a few eccentric cystic lesions and no internal separation or debris. The right fallopian tube is edematous, deformed, and filled with free fluid medially to the previously noted lesion. A pre-op diagnosis of paraovarian cyst was affirmed and an exploratory laparotomy was proposed.Intraoperatively, on the right side, a 13 × 12 × 8 cm hematosalpinx—confirming as the post-op diagnosis—with a total of three coils of torsion was found; it was detorted, clamped, cut, and ligated, completing a partial salpingectomy. On histopathological examination, the gross features showed a single soft tissue mass that was gray–black in color. The cut section exhibits brown fluid and is uniloculated, whereas the external surface was dilated. Microscopic characteristics revealed a fallopian tube with a hemorrhagic, dilated lumen. The mucosa presents little plicae and is bordered by low cuboidal to columnar epithelium, while the wall displays muscular thinning and hemorrhage.The surgery was uneventful (Figs 1 and 2) and there were no postoperative complications and infections; the patient progressed toward recovery.

Discussion

Hematosalpinx is the accumulation of blood in the distended tubal lumen, which is a rare case by itself. Hematosalpinx accompanied with tubal torsion in a young girl makes it even more complex. The average incidence of isolated tubal torsion is 1 in 1500 000 women, therefore being a rare occurrence. It can be noticed during an array of reproductive periods, including youth, pregnancy, and the perimenopausal and menopausal stages [1, 4].The typical etiologies in non-pregnant women are tubal endometriosis, ongoing salpingitis, torsion, and tumors. Non-tubal congenital and acquired obstructive causes include tumors, ablation, adhesions, imperforate hymen, and uterine abnormalities [5–7]. It is essential to distinguish both intrinsic and extrinsic predisposing variables in the etiology of fallopian tube torsion. A tubal tumor, hydro-, pyo-, or hematosalpinx, an exceedingly long tube, ovulatory or premenstrual congestion, and prior sterilization are examples of the former. Ovarian tumors, uterine enlargement resulting from pregnancy or fibroids, adhesion, and trauma are a few such extrinsic factors [4, 8].Tubal torsion can occur in the normal or the diseased lumen, mostly commonly occurring in the latter [9]. Hematosalpinx may have no visible signs or present pelvic pain which may be accompanied by vaginal bleeding or not. Hemoperitoneum and tubal rupture akin to an acute abdomen [10]. In our case, the patient presented with acute lower abdominal pain, with no vaginal bleeding. On examination, an abnormal mass was palpable over the abdomen which was not ruptured.Accurate diagnosis of the condition is crucial as it can be overlooked to other differentials, such as tubal ectopic pregnancy, endometriosis, tubal carcinoma, retrograde menstruation, or uterine cervical stenosis. Ultrasound, computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging are the diagnostic tests performed to confirm the diagnosis. A dilated fallopian tube may be observed on ultrasound, and altered blood products within the tube are frequently observed as homogeneous low-level echoes. Yet, during a CT scan, blood products within a dilated fallopian tube may appear as high attenuation [11]. Along with the radiological diagnostics, laparoscopy is performed, which is considered a gold standard. The young female was diagnosed with a differential of paraovarian cyst, and was first suggested with exploratory laparotomy, by which a precise diagnosis can be confirmed during the surgery.The initial course of action in managing hematosalpinx is to surgically eliminate the diseased fallopian tube (salpingectomy), or if isolating the ovary is not possible, to remove the tubovarial formation. If the formation ruptures, the abdominal cavity is then cleaned. Access by laparoscopy is desirable. The outlook for survival is good with prompt surgical intervention. Salpingectomy improves the success rate of assisted reproductive technologies, which helps the desired conception to start [12]. Anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs, dexamethasone) and enzyme resorption (hyaluronidase) therapy has been suggested during the postoperative period.

Conclusion

The careful management of pelvic organ inflammatory conditions, the practice of personal and intimate cleanliness, and routine preventative exams are all important to prevent the development of hematosalpinx. It is always suggested for regular gynecological visits from an early age to treat and cure any abnormalities to prevent the further development that could hamper with the well-being of an individual.

generate_case.case_summary:
Please provide a detailed medical history of the patient, including chief complaint, history of present illness, past medical history, family history, physical examination, results of ancillary tests, and other necessary information for the final diagnosis in the following format.

- **Patient Information:** 13-year-old female, celibate.
- **Chief Complaint:** Abdominal pain and fever.
- **History of Present Illness:** Two months of intermittent abdominal pain in the right lumbar and iliac areas radiating to the right leg, with associated nausea, vomiting, fever (5 days), and self-palpated enlarging mass.
- **Past Medical History:** Menarche at 11 years old, regular menstrual cycles with dysmenorrhea, clots, burning micturition, and heavy bleeding during periods.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Palpable suprapubic mass in the lower right quadrant of the abdomen; general pallor noted; no extragenital abnormalities or other complications.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Ultrasound: Right ovary with a large 10.9 × 10.00 × 11.1 cm well-defined anechoic cystic lesion, right fallopian tube edematous and deformed with free fluid.
  - Electrocardiogram: Sinus tachycardia.
  - Intraoperative findings: Right fallopian tube with hematosalpinx (13 × 12 × 8 cm) and three coils of torsion, confirming the diagnosis of tubal torsion with hematosalpinx.
  - Histopathology: Hemorrhagic dilated fallopian tube with thinning muscular wall and cuboidal-columnar epithelium.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial List of Potential Diagnoses:**
  1. Paraovarian cyst (pre-op suggestion based on ultrasound findings of a cystic lesion).
  2. Ectopic pregnancy (ruled out due to negative beta-hCG and virgin status).
  3. Endometriosis (unlikely given no classic cyclic symptoms or endometriotic lesions during surgery).
  4. Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) (no evidence of infection or inflammatory changes intraoperatively or histopathologically).
  5. Hematosalpinx with tubal torsion (confirmed intraoperatively).

- **Rationale for Inclusion of Hematosalpinx with Tubal Torsion:**
  - Clinical symptoms of severe, localized abdominal pain.
  - Imaging findings of an abnormal cystic lesion in the right adnexa.
  - Intraoperative findings of a distended, hemorrhagic fallopian tube with torsion.
  - Histopathological features consistent with hematosalpinx.

- **Reasons for Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
  - Paraovarian cyst: Ultrasound findings were initially suggestive, but intraoperative exploration lacked evidence of it.
  - Endometriosis/PID: No intraoperative or histopathological confirmation.
  - Ectopic pregnancy: Negative beta-hCG and patient’s celibate status.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Hematosalpinx secondary to isolated torsion of the right fallopian tube.

results.content:
Alright, let's dive into this case. We've got a 13-year-old female patient presenting with abdominal pain and fever. She's been experiencing these symptoms intermittently for about two months, with the pain located in the right lumbar and iliac areas and radiating to her right leg. Accompanying symptoms include nausea, vomiting, and fever that's been going on for five days now. Additionally, she's noticed a mass that seems to be enlarging.

First, I need to consider her age and gender. She's a teenager, and being female, gynecological issues come to mind, especially since she's had menarche at 11 and has a history of dysmenorrhea, clots, and other menstrual irregularities.

Her chief complaint is abdominal pain and fever, which could indicate a wide range of conditions, from infectious to gynecological to gastrointestinal issues. The fact that the pain is in the right lumbar and iliac areas and radiates to the leg suggests possible involvement of the right lower quadrant, which could point towards conditions like appendicitis, but given the bilateral symptoms and the presence of a mass, that might not be the primary issue.

The history of present illness mentions that the pain has been intermittent for two months, which suggests that this isn't an acute event but something that's been progressing over time. The presence of fever for the past five days indicates an infectious or inflammatory process.

Looking at her past medical history, she has regular menstrual cycles with dysmenorrhea, clots, burning micturition, and heavy bleeding during periods. Dysmenorrhea and clots could be indicative of endometriosis or uterine fibroids, but she's only 13, which makes fibroids less likely. Endometriosis is possible, but it's usually seen in older adolescents and adults.

The physical examination reveals a palpable suprapubic mass in the lower right quadrant, and she has general pallor, which could suggest anemia, perhaps due to heavy menstrual bleeding.

Now, looking at the ancillary tests:

Ultrasound shows a right ovary with a large anechoic cystic lesion, measuring 10.9 x 10.0 x 11.1 cm, which is substantial. The right fallopian tube is edematous and deformed, with free fluid present. This suggests some sort of cystic lesion on the right ovary, possibly a cystadenoma or a cystic teratoma, but given the size and symptoms, it could also be a hemorrhagic cyst.

The ECG shows sinus tachycardia, which could be due to pain, fever, or underlying anxiety.

Intraoperative findings are crucial here: there's a right fallopian tube with hematosalpinx (a collection of blood in the fallopian tube) measuring 13 x 12 x 8 cm, and there are three coils of torsion, confirming the diagnosis of tubal torsion with hematosalpinx.

Histopathology confirms a hemorrhagic dilated fallopian tube with thinning muscular wall and cuboidal-columnar epithelium, which is consistent with the torsion leading to compromised blood supply and subsequent hemorrhage.

So, putting all this together, the patient likely developed a cyst on her right ovary, which caused the fallopian tube to twist, leading to torsion. This torsion cut off the blood supply to the tube, resulting in ischemia and hemorrhage within the tube, causing the hematosalpinx.

The intermittent abdominal pain over two months may have been due to partial torsions that resolved spontaneously, but eventually, it led to a complete torsion, causing persistent pain, fever, and the formation of a mass.

Given that she's 13 and hasn't been sexually active, the risk of pelvic inflammatory disease is low, which makes this gynecological cause more plausible.

In summary, the diagnosis is tubal torsion with hematosalpinx, likely secondary to an ovarian cyst causing the tubal torsion.

### Resoning:

1. **Initial Assessment:**
   - Patient is a 13-year-old female presenting with abdominal pain and fever.
   - Intermittent pain in the right lumbar and iliac areas radiating to the right leg, accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and fever for 5 days.
   - Self-palpated enlarging mass.

2. **Medical History Considerations:**
   - Menarche at 11 with regular menstrual cycles, dysmenorrhea, clots, burning micturition, and heavy bleeding.
   - No personal or family history of note.

3. **Physical Examination Findings:**
   - Palpable suprapubic mass in the lower right quadrant.
   - General pallor suggesting possible anemia.

4. **Ancillary Tests:**
   - **Ultrasound:** Right ovary with a large anechoic cystic lesion (10.9 x 10.0 x 11.1 cm), right fallopian tube edematous and deformed with free fluid.
   - **Electrocardiogram:** Sinus tachycardia.
   - **Intraoperative Findings:** Right fallopian tube with hematosalpinx (13 x 12 x 8 cm) and three coils of torsion.
   - **Histopathology:** Hemorrhagic dilated fallopian tube with thinning muscular wall and cuboidal-columnar epithelium.

5. **Differential Diagnosis:**
   - Ovarian cyst torsion
   - Appendicitis (though less likely given the bilateral symptoms and mass)
   - Pelvic inflammatory disease (low risk due to celibacy)
   - Endometriosis
   - Uterine fibroids (less likely at this age)

6. **Likely Pathophysiology:**
   - Presence of a large ovarian cyst led to tubal torsion, compromising blood supply and causing ischemia and hemorrhage within the fallopian tube, resulting in hematosalpinx.
   - Intermittent pain suggestive of partial torsions that resolved spontaneously, leading to persistent symptoms with complete torsion.

7. **Confirmation from Intraoperative and Histopathological Findings:**
   - Direct visualization of torsion and hematosalpinx during surgery.
   - Histopathological confirmation of hemorrhagic changes and epithelial characteristics consistent with tubal tissue.

### Answer:

Tubal torsion with hematosalpinx due to an ovarian cyst.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial list of potential diagnoses includes paraovarian cyst, ectopic pregnancy, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), and hematosalpinx with tubal torsion.
Step 2: Ectopic pregnancy is ruled out due to negative beta-hCG and virgin status.
Step 3: Endometriosis is unlikely given no classic cyclic symptoms or endometriotic lesions during surgery.
Step 4: PID is excluded as there is no evidence of infection or inflammatory changes intraoperatively or histopathologically.
Step 5: Hematosalpinx with tubal torsion is confirmed intraoperatively based on clinical symptoms, imaging findings, and intraoperative observations.
Step 6: Paraovarian cyst is not confirmed as intraoperative exploration did not find evidence of it despite initial ultrasound findings.
Step 7: Endometriosis and PID are not supported by intraoperative or histopathological findings.
Step 8: Severe, localized abdominal pain, abnormal cystic lesion in the right adnexa, and distended, hemorrhagic fallopian tube with torsion support the diagnosis of hematosalpinx with tubal torsion.
Step 9: Final diagnosis is hematosalpinx secondary to isolated torsion of the right fallopian tube.
